text: Hello dear friends, my name is Dr. Vishal Soni and today we'll be watching a case of robotic cholecystectomy done for symptomatic gallstone disease.
The image displays the anatomy of the extrahepatic biliary system, highlighting the gallbladder, the cystic duct marked for dissection, and the common bile duct which needs preservation.
These are the details of my patient.
The port position that I use is what I call as reverse Aries position as per the constellation of the stars. And this is where we start.
The surgeon begins the dissection at the peritoneum near the neck region of the gallbladder, using robotic instruments to carefully separate the tissue.
The surgeon aims to define Calot's triangle by identifying the junction of the cystic duct and the neck of the gallbladder, focusing on the anatomical structures within this area.
The surgeon identifies the node of Loon and the cystic artery within the surgical field, highlighting these anatomical structures during the robotic cholecystectomy.
The surgeon continues dissection of the peritoneal folds both anteriorly and posteriorly, clearing fibro-fatty tissue around the cystic artery within the Calot's triangle.
The surgeon utilizes various dissection planes around the gallbladder, extending as high as possible to facilitate easier dissection in later stages of the procedure.
The surgeon proceeds with the dissection and incision of the posterior peritoneum near the neck region of the gallbladder, continuing to define the anatomical structures within Calot's triangle.
The surgeon encounters a few small vessels during the dissection, ensuring hemostasis is maintained without significant difficulty.
The surgeon performs further clearance at the Calot's triangle, noting that the cystic duct is positioned more anteriorly than typically expected.

The surgeon performs additional clearance posteriorly, defining the cystic artery and the lymph node of Lund. The dissection around these structures is carefully executed to prevent injury, creating a clear tunnel for further surgical steps.

The assistant uses laparoscopic instruments to apply clips to the cystic artery and cystic duct. The surgeon then divides these structures and continues to separate the gallbladder from the liver bed.

The surgeon navigates through minimal adhesions in the surgical field, smoothly dissecting the areolar tissue to maintain a clear plane for gallbladder separation.
Injuring the gallbladder is fairly easy and we should make sure that we do not do that.
